{{Short description|Abnormal low-impedance connection between two points of different potential, resulting in excessive current flow}}
{{Infobox physical phenomenon
| name = Short circuit
| image = 
| caption = A short circuit is an unintended low-impedance path that bypasses the normal load, causing current to rise to many times the rated value.
| symbols = <math>I_{sc}</math>, <math>I''_k</math> (initial symmetrical short-circuit current), <math>i_p</math> (peak short-circuit current), <math>I_{th}</math> (thermal equivalent short-circuit current)
| SI_unit = [[ampere]] (A) for current, [[volt-ampere|MVA]] for short-circuit power
}}

'''Short circuit''' is an abnormal [[electrical connection]] of very low [[electrical impedance|impedance]] between two points of different [[electric potential|potential]] in a [[electrical circuit|circuit]], whether intended or accidental. The result is an excessive [[electric current]] — the ''short-circuit current'' <math>I_{sc}</math> — limited only by the [[Thévenin's theorem|Thévenin impedance]] of the remaining network. In [[electric power system]]s, short-circuit currents can reach tens of kiloamperes, producing severe thermal and mechanical stresses that must be managed by proper [[power system protection|protection]] and [[switchgear]] design.

== Types of short circuits ==
{{Main|Power system protection}}
In [[three-phase electric power|three-phase]] systems, short circuits are classified by the number of conductors involved and whether [[ground (electricity)|ground]] is contacted:

{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Fault type !! Abbreviation !! Relative frequency !! Phase involvement
|-
| Three-phase (symmetrical) || 3P or L-L-L || ~5% || All three phases, with or without ground
|-
| Line-to-line || L-L or 2P || ~15% || Two phases, no ground
|-
| Line-to-ground || L-G or 1P || ~70% || One phase to ground
|-
| Double line-to-ground || L-L-G or 2P-G || ~10% || Two phases to ground
|}

The three-phase bolted fault (zero fault impedance) produces the highest current magnitude and is used as the basis for [[circuit breaker]] interrupting rating. Line-to-ground faults are the most common but may produce lower current magnitudes, especially in impedance-grounded or isolated-neutral systems.

== Sources of fault current ==
The total short-circuit current at a given location is the sum of contributions from:

* '''Utility grid''' — represented by its Thévenin equivalent (voltage source behind subtransient impedance); provides the steady-state and decaying AC component
* '''[[Synchronous generator]]s''' — contribute subtransient (<math>X''_d</math>), transient (<math>X'_d</math>), and synchronous (<math>X_d</math>) fault current depending on time after fault inception
* '''[[Induction motor]]s''' — act as generators briefly after a fault due to trapped rotor flux, contributing subtransient current for the first 1–3 cycles
* '''[[Synchronous motor]]s''' and condensers — similar to generators, contributing sustained fault current if excitation is maintained

== Symmetrical and asymmetrical faults ==

=== Symmetrical fault current ===
For a bolted three-phase fault, the initial symmetrical short-circuit current <math>I''_k</math> (rms) is:

:<math>I''_k = \frac{c U_n}{\sqrt{3}\, Z_k}</math>

where <math>c</math> is the voltage factor from [[IEC 60909]] (<math>c_{\text{max}} = 1.10</math> for LV, <math>1.10</math> for HV), <math>U_n</math> is the nominal system voltage, and <math>Z_k</math> is the short-circuit impedance at the fault location.

=== DC offset and asymmetry ===
At the instant of fault inception, the current waveform includes a decaying DC component whose magnitude depends on the voltage phase angle at the fault instant. The total asymmetrical current is:

:<math>i(t) = \sqrt{2}\, I''_k\left[\sin(\omega t + \alpha - \phi_k) - \sin(\alpha - \phi_k)\, e^{-t/T}\right]</math>

where <math>\alpha</math> is the voltage phase angle at fault inception, <math>\phi_k = \arctan(X_k/R_k)</math> is the fault impedance angle, and <math>T = L_k/R_k = X_k/(\omega R_k)</math> is the DC time constant.

The '''peak short-circuit current''' <math>i_p</math> (the maximum possible instantaneous value, occurring when <math>\alpha - \phi_k = -\pi/2</math>) is:

:<math>i_p = \kappa \sqrt{2}\, I''_k</math>

where <math>\kappa</math> is the peak factor:

:<math>\kappa = 1.02 + 0.98\, e^{-3R/X}</math>

A typical value for MV/HV systems (<math>X/R \approx 10</math>–<math>30</math>) is <math>\kappa \approx 1.8</math>–<math>2.0</math>, giving <math>i_p \approx 2.5\,\sqrt{2}\,I''_k</math>.

== Short-circuit calculations ==

=== Per-unit system ===
{{Main|Per-unit system}}
Short-circuit studies are performed in the [[per-unit system]], where all impedances are normalized to a common MVA base:

:<math>Z_{\text{pu}} = Z_{\text{actual}} \cdot \frac{S_{\text{base}}}{V_{\text{base}}^2}</math>

The short-circuit MVA (fault level) is:

:<math>S_{sc} = \frac{S_{\text{base}}}{Z_{\text{pu, total}}}</math>

and the symmetrical fault current in per-unit is <math>I_{sc,\text{pu}} = 1 / Z_{\text{pu, total}}</math>.

=== Thévenin equivalent method ===
The network is reduced to a single equivalent voltage source behind a single equivalent impedance as seen from the fault point. This method underpins both manual calculations and computer-based [[power system simulation|short-circuit software]].

=== IEC 60909 method ===
The international standard [[IEC 60909]] defines a systematic procedure using:
* An equivalent voltage source <math>cU_n/\sqrt{3}</math> at the fault location
* Correction factors for generators, power station units, and network transformers
* Calculation of <math>I''_k</math> (initial symmetrical), <math>i_p</math> (peak), <math>I_b</math> (breaking), and <math>I_{th}</math> (thermal equivalent)

== Generator fault contributions ==

{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Reactance !! Symbol !! Time frame !! Typical value (pu) !! Application
|-
| Subtransient || <math>X''_d</math> || First 20–50 ms || 0.10–0.25 || Circuit breaker interrupting duty, peak current
|-
| Transient || <math>X'_d</math> || 50 ms – 1 s || 0.20–0.40 || Relay coordination, voltage dip studies
|-
| Synchronous || <math>X_d</math> || Steady-state (>1 s) || 1.0–2.5 || Sustained fault current, stability studies
|}

The short-circuit current from a generator decays through these stages:

:<math>I_{sc}(t) = \sqrt{2}\,U\left[\left(\frac{1}{X''_d} - \frac{1}{X'_d}\right)e^{-t/T''_d} + \left(\frac{1}{X'_d} - \frac{1}{X_d}\right)e^{-t/T'_d} + \frac{1}{X_d}\right]</math>

where <math>T''_d</math> and <math>T'_d</math> are the subtransient and transient short-circuit time constants.

== Short-circuit MVA ==
The short-circuit MVA (or fault level) at a bus is defined as:

:<math>S_{sc} = \sqrt{3}\, U_n\, I''_k</math>

expressed in [[volt-ampere|MVA]]. It characterizes the "stiffness" of the system at that point — higher fault levels indicate a stronger system with lower source impedance. Typical values range from 250 MVA (weak distribution) to 63,000 MVA (strong 400 kV transmission bus).

== Effects of short circuits ==

* '''Thermal effects''' — <math>I^2t</math> heating (Joule integral) can melt conductors, damage insulation, and weld contacts. The thermal equivalent short-circuit current <math>I_{th}</math> is defined over the fault duration.
* '''Mechanical forces''' — peak current <math>i_p</math> produces electromagnetic forces between conductors proportional to <math>i_p^2</math>. Busbars and [[busbar|busbar supports]] must withstand these forces.
* '''Voltage dip''' — during the fault, voltage at nearby buses sags severely, potentially causing contactor drop-out and [[induction motor|motor]] stalling.
* '''Arc flash''' — an explosive release of energy at the fault point, producing intense heat, light, pressure wave, and molten metal; a major personnel safety hazard governed by IEEE 1584 and NFPA 70E.

== Fault current limiting ==

* '''Current-limiting reactors''' — series [[inductor]]s inserted in feeders or bus-tie positions to add impedance and reduce the prospective fault level
* '''Current-limiting fuses''' — operate in less than half a cycle, limiting <math>i^2t</math> let-through energy
* '''Current-limiting circuit breakers''' — incorporate fast-acting mechanisms and arc chutes that force a rapid increase in arc voltage, limiting the peak current
* '''[[Power transformer|Transformer]] impedance''' — deliberate specification of higher <math>u_k</math> (impedance voltage) to limit secondary fault levels
* '''Is-current-limiting devices''' (Is-limiters) — pyrotechnic or power-electronic devices that insert impedance within microseconds

== Standards ==

* [[IEC 60909]] — Short-circuit currents in three-phase AC systems (calculation method)
* [[IEEE 551]] — IEEE Recommended Practice for Calculating Short-Circuit Currents in Industrial and Commercial Power Systems (Violet Book)
* [[IEEE 141]] — IEEE Recommended Practice for Electric Power Distribution for Industrial Plants (Red Book)
* GB/T 15544 — Chinese standard for short-circuit current calculation (equivalent to IEC 60909)
* IEC 60076-5 — Power transformers: ability to withstand short circuit

== See also ==
{{Portal|Energy}}
* [[Circuit breaker]]
* [[Power system protection]]
* [[Earthing system]]
* [[Electric current]]
* [[Switchgear]]
* [[Protective relay]]
* [[Overcurrent protection]]
* [[Electrical impedance]]
* [[Electric power system]]
* [[Three-phase electric power]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Electrical engineering]]
[[Category:Electric power]]
[[Category:Power system protection]]
[[Category:Electrical safety]]
